element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Players choose from multiple regional daimyōs; foundational choice determines strategic path
Basic economy management; distributing resources between fiefs
Balancing taxation vs. loyalty; rebellion risk increases with high taxes and cultivation
Raising soldier efficiency through training; recruiting ninja for espionage
Turn-based battle system; victory through enemy retreat, command unit destruction, supply exhaustion
Non-aggression pacts and marriage arrangements as strategic alternatives to conquest
Spying on rivals; stealing peasants from rival daimyos
Expanding towns increases tax revenue but decreases peasant loyalty; flood control investment
Buying/selling rice, borrowing funds, purchasing weapons
Ultimate goal: conquest and unification of Japan across 50 provinces

typical_relief_triggers: Recovered health/morale after recruitment and training
Increased peasant loyalty from food distribution
typical_tension_triggers: Enemy military advancement toward player fiefs
Peasant loyalty drops below critical threshold
Incoming battle turn cycle (each turn = 1 day)
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Turn-based seasonal pacing with escalating military tension; campaign scenarios span 1560-1582 (historical Sengoku period), allowing multiple cycles of recruitment, diplomacy, and warfare

spatial_grammar.optional_content_signal: Multiple campaign scenarios available at start; player choice determines region of focus (East, Central, or peripheral expansion)

difficulty_curve.early_game: Low tension; resource and economy management tutorials; peasant loyalty building; no immediate military threats
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Moderate tension; first military conflicts emerge; rivals expand; resource competition increases; need to balance military investment with economy
difficulty_curve.late_game: High tension; multiple rival daimyos compete for remaining provinces; unification requires sustained military campaigns; attrition and supply lines critical
notable_spikes: First declaration of war (shifts from peaceful economy to active warfare)
Multi-front conflicts (defending existing fiefs while expanding)
Late-game rival coalitions (multiple daimyos resisting unification)

notable_level_patterns.description: Four distinct starting scenarios (Battle for the East 1560, Daimyo Power Struggles 1560, Ambition Untamed 1571, Road Towards Unification 1582) allowing players to experience different historical moments with varying strategic difficulty
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Battle for the East (1560) - earliest scenario; most provinces unconquered; requires longest campaign to unification

notable_level_patterns.description: Each controlled province cycles through: resource allocation → peasant management → military recruitment/training → defense/expansion. Multiple fiefs progress in parallel
Early game emphasizes economy and diplomacy; mid-game introduces skirmishes; late-game features major full-scale campaigns with supply/morale considerations

sources_used: wikipedia: Nobunaga's Ambition series overview and gameplay mechanics
